לצאת.text: Your 'o o' landed — the loop works. שורש י־צ־א ('go out'): same root as יְצִיאָה (yetzi'a, the EXIT sign on every Israeli door) and מוֹצָא (motza, origin / way out). Infinitive of pa'al.

כדאי.text: TRAP: don't read this as כְּדֵי (kdei, 'in order to'). כְּדַאי (keday) = 'it's worth it / you should'. Impersonal — no subject: כדאי לך ללכת = 'you should go' (lit. 'it's worthwhile to-you to-go'). You missed it at 95% sure — pure shape-collision with כדי.

אך.text: The formal/literary 'but' — אֲבָל's bookish cousin. Also means 'only / just' (= רק): אך ורק = 'only and exclusively'. One syllable, ach. You read it as אבל at 93% sure; in casual texting אבל is far more common, so seeing אך usually signals a more written register.

מחר.text: MINIMAL PAIR: מָחָר machar = 'tomorrow' vs מַהֵר maher = 'fast/quickly'. Same three slots, only ח↔ה swapped. Tell them apart by the middle letter: ח = tomorroW (time), ה = Hurry. שורש of מחר is opaque; מהר is מ־ה־ר.

מהר.text: MINIMAL PAIR with מָחָר machar 'tomorrow' — only the middle letter differs (ה vs ח). מַהֵר maher = 'fast/quickly', שורש מ־ה־ר ('to hurry', as in מיהר 'he hurried').

משנה.text: You missed this at 89% sure. Casual use is almost always the idiom לֹא מְשַׁנֶּה lo meshane = 'it doesn't matter / whatever' — meshane is the pi'el present of שׁ־נ־ה ('to change'), so literally 'doesn't change (anything)'. Don't confuse the unvoweled spelling with מִשְׁנָה (Mishna).

החוצה.text: Your lowest-confidence word (23%). The ־ָה ending here is the DIRECTIONAL suffix = 'toward': חוץ (outside) → הַחוּצָה (to-outside). Same pattern you already know in הַבַּיְתָה habayta 'homeward' (בית) and קָדִימָה kadima 'forward' (קדם). Spotting that ־ָה tells you motion-toward is happening.

כלומר.text: You missed this twice. כְּלוֹמַר klomar = 'that is / meaning / i.e.' — a discourse connector you use to restate or clarify: 'אני עייף, כלומר אני הולך לישון' = 'I'm tired, meaning I'm going to sleep'. Literally כְּלוֹ-מַר = 'as-if to-say'. On its own, 'כלומר?' = 'so? / meaning what?'

סיבה.text: Missed 3× — your stickiest noun so far. סִבָּה siba = 'reason / cause', שורש ס־ב־ב ('to go around', so a reason is what a thing 'revolves around'). Everyday frames: אין סיבה ('no reason / no need'), מאיזו סיבה? ('for what reason?'), הסיבה היא ש… ('the reason is that…'). Don't confuse with סַבָּא saba 'grandpa'.

אמור.text: Two words, same unvoweled spelling — niqqud splits them: אָמוּר amur = 'supposed to / said' (passive participle, the card's sense), but אֱמֹר emor = 'say! / tell!' (imperative of א־מ־ר). 'אמור להם ללכת' = 'Tell them to go' is the imperative; 'אתה אמור לדעת' = 'you're supposed to know' is amur.

בטח.text: Your first confident miss — 77% sure, wrong. בֶּטַח betach = 'sure / of course / probably' (colloquial). Two everyday uses: agreement, 'בטח!' = 'Sure! / Of course!'; and probability, 'הוא בטח שכח' = 'He probably forgot.' Same root ב־ט־ח as בָּטוּחַ batuach 'safe / certain' and בִּטָּחוֹן bitachon 'security' — the thread is 'with confidence'. Don't read standalone בטח as 'safe'; it's the casual 'sure / probably'.

מעולם.text: Missed it but bet only 26% — good instinct, it barely cost you. מֵעוֹלָם me'olam = 'never', but ONLY for the PAST, and it needs לא: 'מעולם לא הייתי שם' = 'I've never been there.' Literally 'from [the beginning of] the world'. For future/present 'never' use אַף פַּעַם af pa'am or לְעוֹלָם לֹא: 'לעולם לא אסכים' = 'I'll never agree.' Quick test — past → מעולם לא; future → לעולם לא / אף פעם.

לפחות.text: Your stickiest word — wrong on first exposure AND on review, both at low confidence. לְפָחוֹת lefachot = 'at least'. The trap is its bare cousin פָּחוֹת pachot = 'less / fewer' — so the ל is doing all the work: פחות = 'less', לְ+פחות = 'at least' (think 'down to a minimum'). 'לפחות תגיד תודה' = 'At least say thank you'; 'יש פחות אנשים' = 'There are fewer people.' Spot the ל and it flips from 'less' to 'at least'.
